metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact recheck trigger mechanism (post a `recheck` comment via Gerrit REST API using the bot's service account, matching the g2p `comment-added → verify` mapping) and add an explicit 'recheck in-flight' sub-state to the loop that waits for Verified to leave 0 before evaluating the result — the clear-vote job transiently sets Verified=0 at run start, so the bot must not treat Verified=0 as a resolved outcome.
findings.finding: The auto-recheck mechanism has an unspecified trigger path and a hidden state-machine gap: (a) the plan never specifies HOW the bot triggers CI re-execution — in this codebase 'recheck' is triggered by posting a Gerrit comment that fires the g2p comment-added hook → workflow_dispatch (confirmed in gerrit_to_platform.ini.template `recheck = verify` and gerrit-verify.yaml's concurrency group); the bot needs comment-posting permission and must post the exact comment text; (b) after the recheck comment is posted, gerrit-verify.yaml's clear-vote job resets Verified→0 BEFORE the new vote arrives, so the bot's loop will observe Verified=0 mid-run — the plan says 'still shows Verified -1' as the trigger for hand-back, but after a recheck the vote transiently becomes 0, not -1; the bot must not treat the transient 0 as 'recheck complete with no result' nor re-trigger another recheck loop iteration; the plan specifies no intermediate 'recheck in-flight' state for the S2/S3 state machine.
findings.location: AC: CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back); Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC and Outcome record, add: 'The bot triggers recheck by POSTing a comment containing exactly `recheck` on the failing change via the Gerrit REST API (`POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` with `{"message": "recheck"}`). After posting, the loop enters a `recheck_in_flight` sub-state and waits until `Verified` returns to a non-zero value (the clear-vote job transiently resets it to 0; a stable +1 or -1 marks completion of the recheck). Only then is the retry-count incremented and the result evaluated.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC explicitly verifying that `acknowledged` is set to `true` only after `Autosubmit` removal succeeds for **every** change in the stack, and that a simulated mid-removal crash followed by restart results in all remaining changes having `Autosubmit` removed.
findings.finding: The `acknowledged` field lifecycle — writer sets `false` on record, sets `true` after successful `Autosubmit` removal for every change, and on restart any `acknowledged=false` marker re-drives removal — is fully described in the Outcome record section but is not verified by any acceptance criterion. No AC checks that `acknowledged` transitions correctly or that restart re-drives are idempotent.
findings.location: Outcome record section: '`acknowledged`: the writer sets `acknowledged=false` when recording and `true` after `Autosubmit`-removal for every change in the stack completes; on restart, any `acknowledged=false` marker re-drives the removal to completion (idempotent).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the rebase-conflict / CI-fail ACs) specifying when `failing_change_id` is populated in the marker, what value it holds (the change whose rebase returned 409, or the change whose CI vote triggered hand-back), and which consumer reads it.
findings.finding: The marker shape includes a `failing_change_id?` field (Outcome record section) but no acceptance criterion covers when this field is set, what validates its value, or which downstream consumer (S4/S9) reads it. It is an orphaned deliverable element with no verifiable AC.
findings.location: Outcome record section — marker shape definition: `{change_id, patchset_SHA, stack_id, change_ids[], failing_change_id?, tested_sha, recorded_at, acknowledged}`
findings.suggested_fix: In the rebase-conflict AC, state: '`failing_change_id` is set to the `change_id` that returned `409` from `POST /rebase`.' In the CI-fail AC, clarify whether `failing_change_id` is populated (and if not, remove the field from the marker shape to avoid confusion).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific mechanism for the auto-recheck trigger in AC1: specify that the bot posts a `recheck` comment via `POST /changes/{change-number}/revisions/current/review` with `{"message": "recheck"}`, which g2p's `[mapping "comment-added"] recheck = verify` then dispatches to `gerrit-verify.yaml` — and add this endpoint to `infra/autolander/gerrit.py`'s surface list.
findings.finding: The plan's AC1 mandates a 'bounded auto-recheck — 1 retry on the SAME patchset' but never names the Gerrit REST endpoint or mechanism the bot uses to trigger CI re-execution. In this codebase the only supported recheck mechanism is posting a literal 'recheck' comment on the Gerrit change (g2p maps `comment-added` with text 'recheck' to the verify workflow; confirmed in `infra/gerrit/gerrit_to_platform.ini.template` line 43: `recheck = verify`). Comment-posting is a NEW endpoint surface for the bot (S2's gerrit.py uses rebase and submit; it has never posted a comment). Without naming the mechanism, an implementer might invoke GitHub Actions `workflow_dispatch` directly — which bypasses g2p's comment-added mapping and loses Gerrit-side audit trail — or use the Gerrit SSH `gerrit review` CLI instead of the REST API. Evidence class: Partially-verified.
findings.location: AC: CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: In the CI-flake auto-recheck AC, add: 'Trigger: the bot posts a Gerrit review comment `recheck` via `POST /changes/{change-number}/revisions/current/review` body `{"message": "recheck"}`; g2p's `comment-added` mapping re-dispatches `gerrit-verify.yaml` (the same path human operators use). The bot's Gerrit service account credentials (provisioned by S5) are sufficient — any authenticated Gerrit user may post a review message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define in the Outcome record section the precise rule for when `failing_change_id?` is populated (which failure modes set it, which change's ID is recorded, and what absent means to S4/S9 consumers).
findings.finding: The marker schema field `failing_change_id?` is referenced in the Outcome record section but no acceptance criterion or decision rule specifies when this field is populated (ci_failed path only? needs_rebase path? both?), what the absent case means to a consumer, or which downstream component (S4, S9) reads and acts on it — a schema field whose population protocol is completely unspecified.
findings.location: Outcome record section — marker shape: `{change_id, patchset_SHA, stack_id, change_ids[], failing_change_id?, tested_sha, recorded_at, acknowledged}`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: '`failing_change_id?` is set on `needs_rebase` to the `change_id` that received the 409 from `POST /rebase`; it is left null for `ci_failed` (where the failing change is identifiable only from live Gerrit Verified votes). S4/S9 must treat null as "derive from live Gerrit."'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC1 the exact mechanism by which the bot triggers the auto-recheck (e.g., `POST /changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/review` with a comment body of `recheck`, which g2p maps to a `gerrit-verify.yaml` dispatch via the `comment-added` hook).
findings.finding: AC1 specifies 'do a bounded auto-`recheck`' but never defines the mechanism by which the bot triggers CI re-execution. In this codebase, `recheck` is a Gerrit comment-added event: `gerrit_to_platform.ini.template` maps `recheck = verify` under `[mapping "comment-added"]`, meaning a bot comment containing the word `recheck` on the change re-dispatches `gerrit-verify.yaml`. The plan does not specify the Gerrit REST endpoint, comment body, or whether direct `workflow_dispatch` is used instead — leaving the implementer without a specified protocol.
findings.location: AC1 (CI-flake auto-recheck): 'do a bounded auto-`recheck` — 1 retry on the SAME patchset'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'The bot triggers the recheck by calling `POST /changes/{change_id}/revisions/current/review` with `{"message": "recheck"}` — this fires the g2p `comment-added` hook which re-dispatches `gerrit-verify.yaml` (per the `recheck = verify` mapping in `gerrit_to_platform.ini`).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a wait-timeout and fallback to AC1: specify a maximum wall-clock duration (or poll count) the bot waits for the second Verified vote after triggering the auto-recheck, and what it does on timeout (e.g., treat as ci_failed and hand back the stack).
findings.finding: AC1 specifies the bot awaits a second `Verified` vote after triggering the auto-recheck but provides no timeout or fallback for the case where the second CI run never completes (e.g., a hung GitHub Actions runner, an infrastructure outage, or CI cancellation). The plan bounds the retry count (1 recheck) but not the maximum wait duration, leaving an unspecified fallback for an incomplete sub-step that would block the auto-lander loop indefinitely.
findings.location: AC1 (CI-flake auto-recheck): 'only hand the stack back if it fails again'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC specifying exactly when `failing_change_id?` is set (CI-fail only? 409 only? both?) and which consumer reads it, or drop the field until a concrete consumer AC exists.
findings.finding: The marker shape includes `failing_change_id?` (optional field) but zero ACs specify when this field is set, what validates its value, or which downstream consumer reads it. S9 (the only other named downstream besides S4) is archived in the parent epic. This is a premature/unused field with no current done-definition consumer.
findings.location: Outcome record section — marker shape: `{change_id, patchset_SHA, stack_id, change_ids[], failing_change_id?, tested_sha, recorded_at, acknowledged}`
findings.suggested_fix: If the field is only meaningful for CI-fail (to identify which change in the stack triggered the -1), say so explicitly in an AC: 'set to the change_id of the failing change on ci_failed; null on needs_rebase.' If no current consumer requires it, remove it from the marker shape and add it when S4's AC requires it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the file paths and/or ticket IDs for S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) as named consumers of the S3 marker schema, so schema changes have a concrete update list.
findings.finding: S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) are named as consumers of the S3-produced marker/state-dir and marker schema, but neither their file paths nor their ticket IDs are enumerated anywhere in the plan. An implementer cannot determine whether the schema S3 writes is compatible with what these consumers expect, nor which files outside infra/autolander/ must be updated when the marker shape changes.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Consumers' or 'Interface contract' subsection naming the S4 ticket ID (and its expected file, e.g. infra/autolander/land.py or equivalent) and S9 ticket ID, and specify which marker fields each consumer reads.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State whether the '1 retry' bound is enforced per-restart-cycle (in-memory only) or durably across restarts, and if durable, add a `recheck_attempted: bool` field to the marker shape.
findings.finding: The '1 retry on the SAME patchset' bound is ambiguous across restarts: the plan only persists `needs_rebase` markers (not recheck-attempt counts), so the recheck counter is lost on crash/restart, and the bounded retry could be re-driven indefinitely across restart cycles.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck bullet; Outcome record section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the marker shape: `recheck_attempted: bool` (default false, set true before issuing the recheck, checked before deciding to recheck on restart). Then the AC reads: 'issue recheck iff `recheck_attempted=false` for this `change_id+patchset_SHA`; set `recheck_attempted=true` before posting the recheck comment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the recheck POST: if the POST fails transiently, retry the POST (with backoff) before consuming the recheck budget; do not count a failed POST as the recheck attempt.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify behavior if the `recheck` comment POST to Gerrit fails (network error, 5xx). It is unclear whether a failed recheck attempt counts as '0 retries used' (and should be retried), or whether the loop proceeds to hand-back as if the recheck were issued.
findings.location: AC: 'CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)' — '1 retry on the SAME patchset'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'record a `ci_failed` outcome' in the CI-fail AC with unambiguous wording such as 'no marker is written; `ci_failed` is derived live from Gerrit at read time' to distinguish it from the `needs_rebase` persist operation.
findings.finding: The word 'record' is used with two incompatible meanings in the same plan: for `needs_rebase` it denotes a write to the persisted state store, while for `ci_failed` the AC says 'record a `ci_failed` outcome (derived live from Gerrit)' — meaning nothing is persisted and the 'record' is purely the observable Gerrit label state. An implementer reading the two ACs in sequence will be uncertain whether CI-fail requires any write operation.
findings.location: AC: CI-fail ('record a `ci_failed` outcome (derived live from Gerrit)') vs AC: Rebase-conflict ('record a `needs_rebase` outcome') vs Outcome record section ('`ci_failed` … are NOT persisted — they are derived LIVE')
findings.suggested_fix: CI-fail AC revised wording: 'after the auto-recheck still shows `Verified -1`, derive `ci_failed` live from Gerrit (no marker persisted), then remove `Autosubmit` from all changes in the affected stack and stop driving the stack.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reword the CI-fail AC's 'record a ci_failed outcome' to make clear this is a runtime-derived state, not a write to the persisted store (e.g. 'the ci_failed outcome is derived live from Gerrit — remove Autosubmit from all stack changes and stop driving the stack; do NOT write a ci_failed marker to the state dir').
findings.finding: The CI-fail AC says 'record a ci_failed outcome (derived live from Gerrit)' but the Persisted State AC and Outcome Record section both state 'ci_failed is NEVER persisted — derived live from Gerrit at read time.' The word 'record' in the CI-fail AC is ambiguous: an implementer could read it as 'write a ci_failed marker to the state dir,' directly contradicting the no-persist rule.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail bullet: 'record a ci_failed outcome (derived live from Gerrit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the CI-fail AC opening clause from 'record a ci_failed outcome (derived live from Gerrit)' to 'after the auto-recheck still shows Verified -1, remove Autosubmit from all changes in the affected stack and stop driving it; the ci_failed outcome is derived live from Gerrit by S4/S9 — no marker is persisted for this state.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the auto-recheck retry count configurable (e.g. via a named constant or config key) and add a measurement note (e.g. 'default=1; tune after observing flake rates in the first N landing cycles') to ground the chosen default.
findings.finding: The auto-recheck retry count of '1' is a hardcoded heuristic asserted without empirical basis or a measurement plan, and is not marked as configurable — meaning it cannot be tuned if flake rates differ from assumptions without a code change.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'CI-flake auto-recheck' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Expose the retry limit as a named config constant (e.g. `AUTO_RECHECK_MAX_RETRIES = 1`) and add a brief note in the AC or design doc: 'default of 1 chosen to absorb single transient flakes; revisit after 2 weeks of landing data.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE/cleanup path for `needs_rebase` marker files (e.g., delete after `acknowledged=true` is set, or after the change is observed merged/abandoned in Gerrit, with a max-age TTL as a safety net).
findings.finding: The `needs_rebase` marker files in the state dir are never deleted — only 'self-invalidating' (ignored when SHA mismatches). There is no RETIRE/cleanup path, so files accumulate indefinitely: one file per (change_id, patchset_SHA) pair across all stacks and all time.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC: 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache'
findings.suggested_fix: After setting `acknowledged=true` (post Autosubmit-removal loop completes), schedule deletion of the marker file. Alternatively, a periodic sweep deletes markers whose change is merged or abandoned in Gerrit, with a configurable TTL (e.g., 30 days) as a backstop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify timeout and retry/backoff behavior for live Gerrit API reads (e.g., 30 s timeout, 3 retries with exponential backoff), and define the loop's behavior when Gerrit is unreachable (pause with backoff, do not modify any state).
findings.finding: The plan derives `merged`/`ci_failed`/`review_failed` 'live from Gerrit at read time' but specifies no timeout, circuit-breaker, or degraded mode for Gerrit API unavailability. If Gerrit is down or slow, the live-read path in the auto-lander loop has no fallback, and the plan does not state whether the loop should pause, fail-safe, or propagate an error.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'derived LIVE from Gerrit at read time'; Scope: 'Failure/edge paths of the core-loop service'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether structured `loop.py` stdout/stderr log lines (captured by journald) already satisfy the per-transition debug requirement; if so, drop the separate append-file rolling log and rely on journald, or state explicitly why journald is insufficient (e.g., needs cross-restart persistence beyond journald's retention).
findings.finding: The plan mandates 'a rolling per-transition log line is kept for debugging' alongside the single-slot marker file. The existing infra already ships structured logging to journald for the autolander service (evidenced by the review-bot journald pattern in infra/runbooks/review-bot-ops.md and the observability setup in infra/scripts/observability.sh). A separate append-file debug log rebuilds what journald already captures for the same process, without stating why journald is insufficient.
findings.location: Outcome record section — 'A rolling per-transition log line is kept for debugging even though the authoritative record is single-slot.'
findings.suggested_fix: If journald retention is the concern (cross-restart or long-term), note that explicitly and scope the append file to that use-case only (e.g., last-N transitions, bounded by rotation). If journald is sufficient, remove the rolling-log requirement and emit transition events as structured log lines to stdout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or replace 'S9 (batch failure)' as a named consumer of the S3 outcome record — S9 is archived in the parent epic (f1fa); verify which stories (S4 only, or others) actually consume the marker schema and name only those.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'S9 (batch failure)' as a named live consumer of the S3-produced outcome markers ('S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome'), but the parent epic (f1fa-57d8-07cb-44af) explicitly states 'S7-S9 archived; throughput deferred to bounded speculation (idea c3a9-5f4c-bdc6-4e27)' and 'Phase 2 = throughput, DEFERRED'. S9 is an archived/non-existent story, not a live consumer — the scope-exclusion claim that S3's output is consumed by S9 rests on a false premise, which could invisibly delete work (no real downstream will exercise this consumer path).
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome' with 'S4 (land command) consumes this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome' (S9 was archived when cross-stack batching was rejected).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify S9's current status in the parent epic (f1fa-57d8-07cb-44af) and either confirm it is an active consumer (adding its ticket ID) or remove it from the consumer list and name the actual replacement.
findings.finding: The plan names 'S9 (batch failure)' as a live consumer of the S3 marker cache, but prior plan-review analysis of this ticket set notes that S9 was archived in the parent epic (f1fa-57d8-07cb-44af). If S9 is archived/removed, it is a hallucinated consumer reference that will mislead implementers into preserving schema fields no active consumer reads.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that g2p's `comment-added` hook will dispatch `gerrit-verify.yaml` when the **autolander bot service account** posts a `recheck` comment via the Gerrit REST API — confirm no commenter-identity filter in g2p (or in the Gerrit hooks plugin config) silently drops service-account comments, and document the verified behavior in the plan or in ADR-0022.
findings.finding: AC1's 'bounded auto-recheck' relies on the bot posting a literal 'recheck' comment so that g2p's comment-added hook re-dispatches gerrit-verify.yaml — but it is unverified whether g2p will act on a comment posted by the autolander bot service account. Many CI-dispatch integrations (including g2p deployments) include a commenter-identity filter to prevent the bot from triggering itself in a loop; if g2p silently drops the bot's recheck comment, the retry never fires and the stack is handed back on the first flake, defeating the zero-babysitting goal.
findings.location: AC: CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Check the lfit/releng-gerrit_to_platform source (comment-added.py) for any commenter-identity guard. If a guard exists, either (a) allowlist the autolander bot account in the g2p config, or (b) trigger the recheck via a direct Gerrit REST API call to POST /changes/{id}/revisions/{rev}/review (which fires patchset-level events g2p does pick up) rather than a comment post. Document whichever approach is chosen.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state for marker files: specify whether prior-version code must tolerate unknown fields (e.g., via lenient deserialisation), or whether marker files must be purged/migrated before a rollback is performed, so that `Autosubmit` removal can always be completed or safely re-driven.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new persisted marker format (adding fields `acknowledged`, `stack_id`, `change_ids[]`, `failing_change_id?`, `tested_sha`) but does not assert what happens to already-written marker files if the service is rolled back to a prior version that does not know these fields. A prior-version service that reads a new-format marker file may silently ignore unknown fields (leaving `Autosubmit` labels permanently unstripped on the affected stack) or crash, and the plan provides no guidance on forward-compatibility requirements or what schema state exists post-rollback.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a forward-compatibility contract to the marker spec: (1) readers must ignore unknown fields (lenient JSON/YAML parsing), so a rollback to a prior version safely skips `acknowledged`/`stack_id` without crashing; AND (2) document that on rollback, any in-flight `acknowledged=false` markers should be manually cleared or handled by the on-call runbook, since the prior version cannot complete the removal loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command or test reference for each of the seven ACs (e.g., a Gerrit query asserting Autosubmit removal, a log grep for the marker file, a restart simulation asserting idempotent re-drive).
findings.finding: No proving command exists for any of the seven acceptance criteria — the plan describes behavioral requirements but provides zero concrete verification steps, test invocations, or deterministic commands that would produce evidence of correctness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all seven bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: For each AC add a 'Verify:' sub-bullet, e.g.: CI-fail AC → 'Verify: after injecting a synthetic Verified -1 twice, `gerrit query --format JSON change:<id> --current-patch-set | jq -e ".labels.Autosubmit"` returns null for every change in the stack'; Idempotency AC → 'Verify: kill the lander after marker write but before label removal, restart, and assert label is absent and marker `acknowledged=true`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit deliverable (ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc entry) capturing: (1) the 'Gerrit is source of truth, persisted store is a cache' stance, (2) the self-invalidating SHA marker contract, and (3) the acknowledged-flag restart/re-drive protocol.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — the 'persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache, not an authoritative record' pattern, the self-invalidating patchset-SHA marker, and the acknowledged-flag re-drive protocol — with no mention of capturing these in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update.
findings.location: Outcome record section; Acceptance Criteria — 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache' and 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart' bullets

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that marker file writes are atomic (e.g., write to a `.tmp` file then `rename`, or use file locking) to prevent torn writes and TOCTOU races on the `acknowledged` flag.
findings.finding: The write of the `needs_rebase` marker (and the `acknowledged` flag update) is not specified to be atomic. Two concurrent restarts or loop iterations could race: both read `acknowledged=false`, both drive Autosubmit removal (idempotent — safe), but both then attempt to write `acknowledged=true`. More critically, the initial marker write under concurrent triggers (e.g., two loop ticks hitting 409 simultaneously) could interleave without a lock or write-then-rename.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC: 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart'
findings.suggested_fix: Write to `<key>.tmp` then `os.rename()` to `<key>.json` (atomic on POSIX). For the acknowledged update, read-modify-write under a per-key advisory lock or use a single-writer design (only the loop process that recorded the marker drives removal).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements: emit a metric/counter for each `ci_failed` and `needs_rebase` outcome recorded, and for any Autosubmit-removal error, so operators can alert on elevated failure rates.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no metrics, counters, or alerts for the new failure events (ci_failed, needs_rebase, Autosubmit-removal errors). The only observability mechanism named is 'a rolling per-transition log line for debugging', which is insufficient for operators to detect elevated failure rates or alert on them.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'A rolling per-transition log line is kept for debugging'; AC: CI-fail and Rebase-conflict paths

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add test ACs (or a dedicated testing section) covering at minimum: (1) bounded auto-recheck exhausted → full-stack Autosubmit removal; (2) single-flake absorbed by recheck → stack continues; (3) 409 rebase-conflict → needs_rebase marker written + Autosubmit stripped from all stack changes; (4) restart with unacknowledged=false marker re-drives label removal to completion; (5) 404/409 on label-removal API treated as no-op (idempotent strip).
findings.finding: The plan introduces several new failure-path behaviors (bounded auto-recheck with 1-retry logic, Autosubmit label removal from all stack changes on CI-fail or 409, idempotent restart from unacknowledged markers, self-invalidating SHA-keyed persisted markers) but contains zero mention of testing: no test ACs, no test section, no unit/integration/acceptance test work items. The idempotent-under-restart path and the bounded-retry state machine are particularly hard to verify without explicit test coverage described in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all ACs) and Outcome record section — no testing work is described anywhere in the plan
findings.suggested_fix: Add an 'Testing' section with ACs like: 'A unit/integration test simulates Verified -1 on first check, Verified +1 on recheck — confirms stack is not handed back'; 'A test simulates Verified -1 on both checks — confirms needs_rebase marker is written with acknowledged=false and Autosubmit is removed from ALL changes in the stack'; 'A test replays an unacknowledged marker on cold-start — confirms label removal is re-driven and acknowledged flips to true'; 'A test injects 404 on the label-removal call — confirms the loop continues rather than aborting'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the 'S4 already does this' assertion by citing the specific S4 AC or design element that specifies live derivation of `ci_failed`/`merged`/`review_failed`, or add an explicit interface contract between S3 and S4 that pins how each outcome is derived.
findings.finding: The Outcome record section asserts 'S4 already does this' regarding the live derivation of `merged`/`ci_failed`/`review_failed` from Gerrit at read time ('S4 already does this' is the stated basis for NOT persisting those outcomes). S4 is a sibling story in a greenfield epic — `infra/autolander/` does not exist in the repo (confirmed by directory listing and multiple prior plan-review cache entries noting 'dep absent infra/autolander/loop.py'). There is no code in the repo to verify this assertion: S4's live-derivation behaviour cannot be confirmed from the codebase, making this an unverifiable premise that S3's design rests on.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'merged / ci_failed / review_failed are NOT persisted — they are derived LIVE from Gerrit at read time (S4 already does this)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete file (path relative to infra/autolander/) where the needs_rebase marker read/write logic will live, so S4 and any other consumers have a stable import/read target.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a new 'project-infra state dir' for persisting needs_rebase markers but names no concrete file path for the persistence module. Only infra/autolander/loop.py is the implicit edit target (inherited from S2), but marker read/write logic is non-trivially a separate concern. If the persistence is a new file (e.g. infra/autolander/state.py or similar), it is unnameed and thus unenumerated as a new artifact.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'The bot persists ONLY needs_rebase markers in the auto-lander state dir (project-infra state, NOT a rebar event)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a file entry such as 'infra/autolander/state.py (NEW) — needs_rebase marker persistence: read/write/invalidate logic' or confirm that all persistence lives in loop.py.

findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new user-facing failure-handling flows (CI-fail auto-recheck, 409 rebase-conflict hand-back, idempotent restart recovery, Autosubmit label removal with 404/409 no-op) but contains zero testing content anywhere — no unit tests, integration tests, test scenarios, or acceptance-test strategy are described for any of these paths, including the failure/timeout/edge paths the plan itself centers on.
findings.location: Entire plan — Acceptance Criteria section, all ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Testing' section to the plan with: unit tests for the retry-count boundary (exactly 1 retry), a contract test asserting `allow_conflicts` is never set on a rebase call, a table-driven test for label-removal idempotency (200/404/409 responses), a simulated-restart test verifying an `acknowledged=false` marker re-drives removal, and an integration/e2e acceptance criterion that fires a real (or real-shaped) Gerrit 409 response and asserts full-stack Autosubmit removal — this prevents proxy-validation from hiding live-path breakage.

findings.finding: The AC1 conditional 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' introduces conditional behavior gated on a signal distinction that does not exist in the current CI vote surface: gerrit-verify.yaml casts a flat binary Verified +1/-1 (via im-open/workflow-conclusion → WORKFLOW_CONCLUSION), with no infra-vs-test annotation on the Gerrit label. The condition is either permanently false (making it dead code) or requires an unspecified GitHub Actions API call to read run-level job conclusions — which is undescribed scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back), AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' with 'Retry once on any Verified -1 on the same patchset; the binary vote surface provides no infra/test distinction.' This matches what the CI system actually exposes and preserves the zero-babysitting goal.

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion has a concrete proving command — the plan contains zero test commands, integration checks, or deterministic verification steps. Every AC is a behavioral statement with no 'how to verify done' anchor, violating E6(a) and E6(c).
findings.location: All 7 Acceptance Criteria bullets

findings.finding: The AC1 conditional 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' assumes a machine-readable failure-type signal that does not exist in the current Gerrit/CI integration. The actual gerrit-verify workflow casts a single undifferentiated `Verified -1` for all failure modes (test failures, infra errors, network timeouts). The vote message carries only a run URL, not a structured failure-type tag readable from the Gerrit REST API. There is no specified mechanism (label value, vote message parsing rule, or secondary signal) by which the bot could distinguish infra from test failure, making this branch of the retry logic unimplementable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (AC1): 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the conditional with unconditional single retry: 'on a post-rebase Verified -1, issue one auto-recheck on the same patchset before recording ci_failed and stripping Autosubmit.' The infra/test distinction can be added in a follow-on story once the CI infrastructure exposes a structured failure-type signal.

findings.finding: The plan asserts "Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures" without verifying that the actual CI/Gerrit integration provides a machine-readable distinction between infra and test failures. If the CI system emits only a single Verified -1 vote with no sub-type signal, the conditional is silently a no-op (always retry, or never filter), breaking the zero-babysitting goal unpredictably. The 1-retry bound is also a threshold asserted without any measurement of actual CI flake rates to justify it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-implementation spike: query recent Gerrit Verified -1 events in the target project, inspect the vote message/metadata format, and check whether the CI system sets distinguishable fields. Record the outcome in the plan; if no distinction is available, simplify the AC to "always retry once on Verified -1" and add a TODO to revisit when the CI system is upgraded.

findings.finding: The AC specifies 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures', but neither the plan body nor the AC provides an observable mechanism for HOW this distinction is made (e.g., which Gerrit vote label, CI job field, or message pattern signals infra vs test failure), nor what the fallback behaviour is when the distinction is indistinguishable. This leaves a non-deferred goal without an end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck bullet: 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under the CI-flake AC: 'Infra-failure is identified by [specific signal, e.g., a `Verified -1` vote whose message matches /infrastructure|timeout|quota/i, or a designated CI job category]. When the signal is absent or ambiguous, default to [retry once / do not retry] to avoid false positives.'

findings.finding: The CI-flake auto-recheck AC asserts conditional behavior 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' — but the actual CI integration (`gerrit-verify.yaml` + `im-open/workflow-conclusion`) exposes only a single binary `WORKFLOW_CONCLUSION` (success/failure/cancelled) value, and the `Verified` label is a single -1..0..+1 integer with no sub-channel for failure category. No mechanism in the current codebase allows the autolander to distinguish an infra-failure `Verified -1` from a test-failure `Verified -1`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)

findings.finding: AC1 conditions selective retry on 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' — but the actual CI system (gerrit-verify.yaml) casts only a binary `Verified +1` or `Verified -1` label (from `WORKFLOW_CONCLUSION`). No structured infra-vs-test failure sub-type is conveyed via the Gerrit label or any machine-readable side channel back to the autolander. The plan asserts a conditional capability that the current CI-to-Gerrit integration does not provide, making the infra-failure branch of AC1 unimplementable without additional work.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)

findings.finding: The CI-flake AC specifies conditional retry logic — 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' — but neither the plan nor any referenced schema/enum defines a machine-readable mechanism for making this distinction. The actual Verified label (verified in infra/gerrit/project.config and infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md) is a scalar -1/0/+1 with an optional freetext message URL; there is no structured failure-type field, no enum, and no API surface that exposes infra-failure vs test-failure to a consumer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) specify that the current CI integration casts a single scalar Verified -1 with no structured failure-type, so the retry is unconditional (simpler, matches actual capability), or (b) define an explicit enum/schema field (e.g., parse the GitHub Actions `conclusion` field via the URL in the Verified vote message) and co-locate the parsing protocol with the marker schema.

findings.finding: The AC-1 conditional 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' references a signal/vocabulary that is never defined anywhere in the plan. The gerrit-verify.yaml workflow casts a single integer Verified vote (-1/0/+1) with no sub-type field; adapter.py's _TAG_SUFFIXES coverage-gap sub-reasons apply only to LLM-Review message bodies, not to Verified. The plan provides no enum of infra-failure indicators (exit code, job name pattern, message substring, or separate Gerrit label), no decision rule for classifying a -1 as infra vs test, and no fallback behavior for the common case when the signal is absent or indistinguishable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (AC-1)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) specify that the bot inspects the GitHub Actions run conclusion for each job by calling `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/runs?...` and classifying any run where only infrastructure jobs (e.g., matching `runner-setup`, `clear-vote`) failed as infra-failure, with a defined pattern list; or (b) simplify to 'always retry once on any Verified -1' and remove the conditional, since the infra/test distinction cannot be reliably read from the Gerrit vote alone.

findings.finding: The CI-flake AC conditions retry behavior on whether 'the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure' but never specifies the distinguishing mechanism — which Gerrit label value, which CI job name pattern, or which API field signals infra-failure vs. test-failure. An executor cannot implement this conditional without investigation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck bullet: 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures.'
findings.suggested_fix: State a default: 'If the CI system does not provide a structured infra-vs-test signal, retry ALL Verified -1 once (the 1-retry bound still limits blast radius). If the Gerrit vote message contains a bot-posted structured tag such as `INFRA_FAILURE`, limit retry to those.' This turns the open conditional into an actionable decision with a safe default.

findings.finding: The AC-1 conditional 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' references a signal that does not exist in the current gerrit-verify.yaml pipeline: all CI failures cast an undifferentiated `Verified -1` via a single `WORKFLOW_CONCLUSION` env var with no structured infra/test tag. The conditional branch that would 'only retry infra-failures' is permanently vacuous today — making it a YAGNI hypothetical serving a future CI signal, not the current done-definition.
findings.location: AC: CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' with 'retry once on any `Verified -1`' — matching the actual undifferentiated signal. If infra-tagging is planned, capture it as a separate future story rather than a conditional that silently does nothing today.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
